macro_segments.title: Attribution of the Mishnah to Rabbi Akiva or Rabbi Tarfon and the Role of Admission or False Oath

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's statement about theft with false oath and the obligation to travel to Madai
Connection to the dispute between Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Tarfon about unknown victim
The Gemara's proposal that the Mishnah follows Rabbi Tarfon's view with a higher liability when oath is sworn
The connection through the biblical verse about guilt offering and the obligation to return to the proper owner
Rabbi Akiva's position that higher liability applies even without an oath
The Braita showing Rabbi Tarfon agrees with Rabbi Akiva when the thief admitted the theft

macro_segments.title: Rava's Alternative Explanation: The Thief as Shomer Pikadon Rather Than Gazlan

micro_segments.title: Rava's rejection of comparing the Mishnah to the dispute between Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Tarfon
The distinction that the Mishnah deals with a case where the thief knows who he stole from
Once the thief admits and knows the victim, he transforms from gazlan to shomer pikadon
If the thief takes an oath while holding the money as shomer, he violates the terms and must atone
If no oath is taken, he remains merely a shomer watching money until owner retrieves it

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rav Chisda and Rabba Regarding Shaliach Made with Witnesses

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's prohibition against giving money to a shaliach, and the question of what status a shaliach made with witnesses has
Rav Chisda's position that a shaliach made with witnesses counts as a proper shaliach
Rabba's position that even with witnesses a shaliach does not count as a legal representative
The Braita in Bava Metzia about borrowing a cow and sending with shaliach as a proof for Rabba
Rav Chisda's response that the Mishnah refers to a hired worker or sharecropper, not a true shaliach
The distinction between shaliach made by negzal versus by gazlan

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Elazar on Shaliach Made with Witnesses

micro_segments.title: Both Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Elazar agree that a shaliach made with witnesses counts as a valid shaliach
Explaining the Mishnah as referring to a casual appointment, not an official shaliach

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Position on Sending Money with Dyuqani and the Requirement of Hitkabalti

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's ruling that money cannot be sent with a dyuqani even if it has witnesses' signatures
Rabbi Yochanan's contrary opinion that if witnesses sign the authorization it is valid
The story of Rabbi Abba and the requirement for hitkabalti before releasing money
Rava bar Rav Yosef's objection that even hitkabalti doesn't work if the creditor dies before payment
The solution using kinyan agav to transfer money via land acquisition
The case of Rav Papa using this method to transfer 13,000 zuzim

macro_segments.title: Whether Chomesh (Twenty-Five Percent Penalty) is Mammon or Knas and Transmission to Heirs

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah indicating the thief must travel to Madai even after paying principal to pay chomesh
Inference that chomesh is monetary property since heirs must pay it and interest can compound
The Braita about heirs paying principal and chomesh but exempt from bringing guilt offering

macro_segments.title: When Sons Must Pay Principal and Chomesh for Father's Theft Based on Admission and Oath

micro_segments.title: The Braita about circumstances when a son must pay chomesh for the father's theft
Rav Nachman's explanation that admission versus non-admission affects the obligation
The distinction that only if one of them swore must chomesh be paid
Alternative Braita discussing when principal is owed even without oath
The cryptic phrase 'Yesh Talmud' and its meaning as a Midrash Halacha inclusion
Rav Huna's clarification of when sons are liable for father's theft
Rava's example of a closed saddlebag of the father in someone else's control

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Akiva vs Rabbi Tarfon - theft from multiple victims
Madai - far distant place for return of theft
Rav Chisda vs Rabba - shaliach with witnesses
Rav Yosef bar Chama - creditor in money dispute
Rabbi Abba - debtor seeking to repay
Rava bar Rav Yosef - son challenging payment terms
Rav Papa - example of kinyan agav transaction
Yesh Talmud - learned from textual interpretation
Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Elazar - agree on shaliach
